subject: ap-chemistry
title: Chemical Equilibrium
stimulus:
The ionization of HF(aq) in water is represented by the equation above. In a 0.0350 M HF(aq) solution, the
percent ionization of HF is 13.0 percent.

parts.prompt:
Two particulate representations of the ionization of HF molecules in the 0.0350 M HF(aq) solution are
shown below in Figure 1 and Figure 2. Water molecules are not shown. Explain why the representation of
the ionization of HF molecules in water in Figure 1 is more accurate than the representation in Figure 2.
(The key below identifies the particles in the representations.)
parts.scoring_notes: Percent ionization = 13.0%, meaning only 13% of HF molecules are ionized; 87% remain as HF molecules. Figure 1 correctly shows mostly intact HF molecules with a smaller number of H$_3$O$^+$ and F$^-$ ions (consistent with 13% ionization). Figure 2 incorrectly shows complete or near-complete ionization (mostly ions). Since HF is a weak acid with partial ionization, Figure 1 is more accurate.

scoring_points.description: For explaining that only 13% ionization means mostly HF molecules remain (Figure 1 shows this correctly): 1 point

required_elements: Percent ionization = 13.0% — only 13% of HF has ionized; 87% remains as HF.
Figure 1 shows mostly HF molecules with fewer ions, consistent with 13% ionization. Figure 2 incorrectly shows too many ions (appears to show complete ionization).
alternatives.correct_example: HF is a weak acid. At 13.0% ionization, only 13% of HF molecules dissociate into H$_3$O$^+$ and F$^-$; the remaining 87% are intact HF molecules. Figure 1, which shows a majority of HF molecules and a small number of ions, accurately reflects this partial ionization. Figure 2 shows an equal or greater number of ions than molecules, implying near-complete ionization — inaccurate for a 13% ionization.

wrong_examples: Saying Figure 2 is more accurate because HF is an acid that should ionize fully — HF is a WEAK acid, ionizing only 13%; it is not a strong acid.
Citing the wrong figure as correct — Figure 1 (mostly molecules) is accurate for 13% ionization.
common_traps: Weak acid = partial ionization. 13% ionization → at equilibrium, 87% of molecules intact. The particulate diagram must show more HF molecules than H$^+$/F$^-$ ions.

parts.prompt:
Use the percent ionization data above to calculate the value of $K_{a}$
for HF.

scoring_points.description: For the correct [H$_3$O$^+$] from percent ionization: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using 0.130 M as [H$_3$O$^+$] — 13.0% is a percent, not a concentration. Multiply by the initial [HF].
Using [HF]$_0$ in the denominator instead of [HF]$_{eq}$ — the equilibrium expression uses equilibrium concentrations, not initial.
Not squaring [H$_3$O$^+$] in the numerator — $K_a = [H^+][F^-]/[HF]$ and [H$^+$] = [F$^-$], so numerator = [H$^+$]$^2$.
common_traps: K$_a$ expression: equilibrium concentrations only. [HF]$_{eq}$ = [HF]$_0$ − [H$^+$]. Don't forget to subtract the ionized amount from [HF].

parts.prompt:
If 50.0 mL of distilled water is added to 50.0 mL of 0.035 M HF(aq), will the percent ionization of HF(aq)
in the solution increase, decrease, or remain the same? Justify your answer with an explanation or
calculation.
parts.scoring_notes: Increase. Adding water dilutes the solution: [HF] decreases from 0.0350 M to 0.0175 M (halved). Dilution decreases all concentrations, so Q = [H$^+$][F$^-$]/[HF] < K$_a$. The system shifts right to reach equilibrium. In the new equilibrium, a greater fraction of HF is ionized (higher % ionization), even though the absolute concentrations are lower. This is a fundamental property of weak acid equilibria: dilution increases percent ionization.

scoring_points.description: For 'increase' with Q < K$_a$ Le Chatelier justification or calculation: 1 point

required_elements: Dilution decreases all concentrations, Q < K$_a$ → reaction shifts right → more HF ionizes → higher % ionization. OR: new [HF]$_0$ = 0.0175 M; new [H$^+$] at equilibrium is less than 0.00455 M but % ionization = [H$^+$]/0.0175 > 13.0%.
alternatives.correct_example: Increase. Adding 50.0 mL water to 50.0 mL of 0.0350 M HF halves the concentration of all species. Q = [H$^+$][F$^-$]/[HF] decreases (numerator decreases as the square, denominator halves → Q decreases by factor of 2). Since Q < K$_a$, equilibrium shifts to the right (more ionization). The percent ionization increases because the equilibrium favors ionization at lower concentrations.

wrong_examples: Saying percent ionization decreases — dilution of a weak acid always INCREASES percent ionization, even as absolute concentration decreases.
Saying percent ionization stays the same — K$_a$ is constant, but % ionization changes with dilution.
common_traps: K$_a$ is constant at constant temperature. Diluting a weak acid solution forces more ionization per original molecule → % ionization increases. The total [H$^+$] is lower, but the fraction ionized is higher.
